texts.label: text
texts.orig: Lithium bromide solution, the working fluid in absorption chillers, is rendered unusable when contaminated via a high concentration of sodium ions. In the present paper, the results of a study on the recovery of lithium ions as lithium hydroxide from sodium-contaminated solution of lithium bromide by batch electrodialysis (ED) process are presented. A fourcompartment ED cell was designed and built with the aim of investigating the effects of operating parameters of applied voltage and lithium ion concentration in the feed solution on some objective functions, including, importantly, lithium ions' recovery, specific electricity consumption, and current efficiency. The obtained results showed that recovery of lithium ions as lithium hydroxide and current density were enhanced by increasing voltage applied. However, using high voltages led to higher specific electricity consumption because of irreversible energy dissipation resulting from heat generation. In addition, decreasing the initial feed concentration led to higher observed values for lithium hydroxide recovery, the ratio of lithium-to-sodium ions' concentrations in the cathodic compartment, and specific electricity consumption. By applying the ED process to a contaminated solution containing 1.9857 M of lithium ions and 0.0587 M of sodium ions, the resultant cathodic solution had a lithium-to-sodium ion concentration ratio of 88.5, for an applied voltage of 7 V. This ratio for fresh and unpolluted lithium bromide solution is 58, which means the applied method can be considered as a promising method to recover the polluted LiBr solution.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In absorption chillers, contamination of lithium bromide solution by sodium ions makes the solution unsuitable for use as working fluid. Complete removal of sodium ions from the contaminated lithium bromide solution is not possible because lithium and sodium ions have similar chemical properties. The main goal of the present paper was relative separation of the mentioned ions and recovery of lithium ions in the form of lithium hydroxide from the contaminated, and therefore, waste stream of the lithium bromide solution.

texts.orig: ED is an electrochemical membrane separation process in which ions pass through an ion-selective semipermeable membrane from one compartment (diluted compartment) to another (concentrated) compartment by applying an electrical field as the separation driving force (Strathmann, 2010). This membrane technology has attracted much attention from academia and industry because of its diversity, simple design and control, sophisticated performance, and technological compatibility (Saremirad et al., 2012). The key components of an ED cell are its ion exchange membranes, which are arranged, alternately, in a direct current field between two electrodes, namely cathode and anode, and the electrolyte is partitioned into the compartments by these membranes (Saremirad et al., 2012; Abou-Shady et al., 2012a). Upon applying the electrical potential to the ED cell, positiveor negative-charged particles in the electrolyte (dissolved salt) migrate toward to the electrodes with the opposite charge (Zhang et al., 2012a; Kabay et al., 2008). The consequence of ions' movement through the membranes will increase the ionic concentration in some compartments and deplete ions in the adjacent compartments. As a result, concentrated and diluted streams are formed (Abou-Shady et al., 2012a; Zhang et al., 2012a; Masigol et al., 2012). ED process has been used extensively to produce and to process water, to recover sodium chloride from sea and brackish water, to remove ionic species and useful materials from industrial effluents, in salt production, and in the deashing of milk whey (Strathmann, 2010; Kabay et al., 2008; Masigol et al., 2012; Jing et al., 2012; Sadrzadeh and Mohammadi, 2009; Zhang et al., 2007). ED has also been studied for many other applications, such as producing inorganic acids, recovering organic acids, concentrating industrial feed or effluents, and removing harmful ions, such as F -, NO3 -, Cl -and Br -and heavy metals such as Pb 2+ , Ni 2+ , Cu 2+ , Cr 3+ , Mn 2+ , and Fe 3+ . Additional functions include separation of protein and mixture of amino acids in the process of vitamin C production, and, recently, for generating electricity (Zhang et al., 2012a; Mulyati et al., 2012; Luo et al., 2002; Scialdone et al., 2012; AbouShady et al., 2012b). Further specific ED processes include recovering ionic liquid from diluted aqueous solution (Wang et al., 2012), recovering l -malic acid from beverage industry wastewater (Lameloise and Lewandowski, 2012), soy sauce desalting (Fidaleo et al., 2012), and recovering hydrochloric acid from simulated chemosynthesis aluminum foils wastewater (Zhang et al., 2012a). Further examples of uses for the ED process include removal and recovery of Cr (VI) from wastewater (Xing et al., 2009), regeneration of sodium hydroxide spent caustic (Wei et al., 2012), evolution of cranberry juice physicochemical parameters during phenolic antioxidant enrichment (Bazinet et al., 2012), separation of divalent from monovalent ions (Zhang et al., 2012b), and production of ionic liquids (Haerens et al., 2012). Further uses include separation of fluoride from aqueous solution (Kabay et al., 2008), analysis of the sodium lactate concentration (Boniardi et al., 1996), concentration of formic acid solution (Luo et al., 2002), treatment of seawater (Sadrzadeh and Mohammadi, 2009), and recovery of glutamic acid from isoelectric acid (Zhang et al., 2007).

texts.orig: · In the ED process, the initial concentration of the species affects the rate of ions' transfer through the membranes. In the system under investigation in this work, the concentration of Li + ions in the feed solution is greater than that of Na + ions (3.9714 M compared with 0.1261 M), and therefore the Li + transfer rate is expected to be higher than that of sodium ions.

texts.orig: 2.1. ED set up and membranes
texts.text: 2.1. ED set up and membranes

texts.label: text
texts.orig: To carry out the experiments, a four-compartment ED cell was designed and built. The ED cell was made of Plexiglas for its proper resistance to acid, base, and feed solutions existing in the cell. Cation and anion exchange membranes alternately separated the cell compartments. The effective areas of the membranes were 9.5 cm × 4.5 cm and all compartments had equal thickness of 10 mm. The anion and cation exchange membranes used in this work were AR204SXR412 and CR67, MK111 (Ionics, MA, USA), both supplied by Arak Petrochemical Complex. Table 1 lists the properties of the membranes. Prior to each experiment, pre-treatment of membranes was conducted to expel impurities and stabilize them. For this purpose, anion exchange membrane (AEM) and CEM were dipped in NaOH (1 M) and HCl (1 M) solutions, respectively, for 2 h, and then washed thoroughly with distilled water. By this means, the AEM and CEM were converted to hydroxide and hydrogen forms, respectively. Platinum-coated titanium and nickel mesh, both provided by Niroo Chlor Co. (Iran), were used as anode and cathode, respectively. The area of each electrode was 9.5 cm × 4.5 cm. By using an adjustable DC power source (Star 305, Iran), direct electrical current was applied to the cell. The power source supplied the desired voltage and direct current in the range of 0-40 V and 0-4 A, respectively. The experiments were run at constant voltage mode. Fig. 1 displays the schematic diagram of the experimental set up for ED.

texts.orig: rinse solutions were filled with 1 l of 0.02 M H2SO4 and 0.5 l of a solution containing of 2.46 mM NaOH and 0.1416 mM LiOH, as anodic and cathodic electrode rinse solutions, respectively. In addition, the acid compartment reservoir tank was fed with 1 l of 0.02 M HCl solution and the feed compartment reservoir tank was filled with 1 l of contaminated LiBr solution taken from central heating station of Isfahan University of Technology, Isfahan, Iran. The concentration of feed differed for each type of experiment. Fresh solutions filled the reservoir tanks for each experiment, and proper centrifugal pumps drove these solutions through the compartments. After adjusting the voltage to the desired value, the process began. All experiments were run for 360 min. Samples of cathodic solution were taken from the cathodic reservoir every 120 min. The concentration of Li + and Na + ions were determined by Inductively Coupled Plasma (ICP-OES) device. ICP/OES is a powerful technique for the determination of metals concentration in a variety of different sample matrices. This technique is based upon the spontaneous emission of photons from atoms and ions that have been excited in a RF discharge. Detail explanations about this analytical method are given in the literature (Hou and Jones, 2000).

These factors are lithium hydroxide recovery, specific electricity consumption, lithium ions' permeation flux, current efficiency for the lithium ions, the ratio of lithium-to-sodium ion concentrations in the cathodic compartment, the ratio of lithium ions' concentration in the cathodic chamber at any time over the initial concentration ( C / C 0), and current density. The subsections below explain the calculation methods.

texts.orig: Fig. 2 reveals that with increasing applied voltage, the recovery and current density were increased. This can be explained by the fact that, increasing the applied voltage and the driving force for transporting ions through the membranes leads to higher lithium ion recovery and current density. According to the above reason with increasing applied voltage ions permeation flux was increased. Also, electricity consumption was increased, for different concentrations, by increasing applied voltage. This occurs because a greater part of total electrical energy was consumed to overcome the electrical resistance, and irreversible energy dissipation was generated in the form of heat as the voltage increased (Wei et al., 2012; Wu et al., 2011). According to the definition of SEC, this parameter is directly proportional to the created electrical potential difference and changes of the electrical current with time, while it declines with the number of moles that were removed from the feed compartment. It was observed that, by increasing applied voltage, the effect of enhancement of the created electrical potential and changes of electrical current with time conquer the reverse effects of the number of the moles removed from the feed compartment. Consequently, SEC was increased. Fig. 3 provides the ratio of lithium and sodium ions' concentration over initial concentration ( C / C 0) and the ratio of lithium-to-sodium ions' concentrations in the cathodic compartment at any time for all values of applied voltage. As can be seen, this figure shows that by applying voltage of 7 V, the C / C 0 parameter of lithium for the feed with the higher initial concentration increased from 1 to 445 after 360 min. This parameter increased from 1 to 320 for the feed with lower concentration. Also, the ratio of lithium to sodium concentration in the cathodic compartment increased from 1 to about 70. Fig. 3b confirms that lithium ions were transferred more rapidly than sodium ions into the cathodic compartment, and relative separation of the two metal ions was achieved. The ratio of 88.5 (maximum ratio) was obtained at a voltage of 7 V for the feed with Cf/2 concentration after 360 min, while this ratio for contaminated solution was about 9. By increasing applied voltage, the concentration of lithium ions in the cathodic compartment and ratio of lithium-to-sodium ions' concentrations were increased. As previously mentioned, by increasing applied voltage, ions' permeation flux in the cathodic compartment was augmented. Furthermore, according to the three reasons discussed in the introduction, lithium ions' permeation toward the cathodic compartment is greater than that of the sodium ions. Therefore, the ratio of lithium-to-sodium ions' concentrations was increased. Fig. 4 shows the effect of applied voltage on current efficiency. texts.orig: 3.2. Effect of feed concentration
texts.text: 3.2. Effect of feed concentration

texts.label: text
texts.orig: To study the effects of initial concentration of lithium ions in the feed solution, experiments were performed with two different values of concentration (Cf:3.9714M Li + and Cf/2:1.9857 M Li + ) for 360 min. Effects of feed concentration on LiOH recovery and cell current density are given in Fig. 5. As can be seen from Fig. 5a, the recovery percentage of about 20% was achieved after 360 min at the lower feed concentration (Cf/2). Moreover, Fig. 5a reveals that by increasing the initial feed concentration, the recovery was reduced. This can be explained by the fact that with increasing the feed concentration, cell conductivity and consequently the number of the transferred ions are increased. However, the number of ions that can pass through the membranes is limited. As previously described, recovery is defined by dividing the amount of migrated ions from the diluted to the cathodic chamber during each experiment by the initial number of ions in the diluted reservoir tank. In spite of the rise in the number of lithium ions transferred by increasing the initial feed solution concentration, recovery was reduced because of the high number of initial lithium ions in the feed solution (Ben Sik Ali et al., 2010; Sadrzadeh and Mohammadi, 2008). Furthermore, as seen in Fig. 5a, by reducing the initial feed concentration from Cf to Cf/2 at a constant voltage, recovery increment was less than twice as large. Once the feed solution concentration is reduced to half of its initial value, despite the denominator in the fraction that defines the recovery becoming half of its initial value, the numerator of the fraction is reduced to a value less than its initial value. Therefore, the recovery factor is less than twice its initial concentration. By increasing the feed concentration, the cell current density and both permeation fluxes of lithium and sodium ions became larger. Variation of current density with time for different applied voltages is shown in Fig. 5b. This occurs because at the higher concentration, the total electrical conductivity of the cell was higher and therefore, at the constant applied voltage, current density and ions' transport increased (Parulekar, 1998). According to the diagrams for the sodium and lithium ions' permeation flux, the large difference between the fluxes of the sodium and lithium ions verifies the achievement of the project's goal, i.e., the relative separation of lithium ions from sodium ions. However, at the voltage of 3 V, because of the low driving force, the permeation fluxes were almost constant. By increasing the initial feed concentration at the applied voltages of 5 and 7 V,

texts.orig: the SEC parameter was reduced. In fact, by increasing feed concentration, either the number of the ions or conductivity in the feed compartment is increased. Consequently, cell total resistance and the necessary electrical energy to overcome the electrical resistance become smaller than previously noted. However, for the applied voltage of 3 V, the SEC did not show any significant change with increased feed concentration. Perhaps at 3 V the ion permeation flux is inherently low due to relatively weak driving force and, therefore, changing the feed concentration does not have a significant effect on the number of transferred ions. Time changes in the ratio of lithium ion concentration in the cathodic compartment over its initial value ( C / C 0) for different initial concentrations of the feed solution are given in Fig. 6a. This figure reveals that increasing feed concentration leads to higher C / C 0 ratio values at any applied voltage, owing to the increased conductivity of the feed solution and therefore the higher ion permeation flux. The variation of the ratio of lithium-to-sodium ions' concentrations in the cathodic compartment with respect to process duration is shown in Fig. 6b. As demonstrated in Fig. 6b, decreasing the feed concentration had positive effect on increasing the above-mentioned ratio, which was in favor of the research goal, i.e., relative separation of lithium ions from sodium ions. This effect can be explained by the fact that decreasing the concentration of feed solution led to higher cell electrical resistance and consequently declining ions permeation fluxes toward the cathodic compartment. It has to be emphasized that the net effect of reduced permeation fluxes was in favor of lithium ions, owing to their having higher concentration in the feed solution with respect to the sodium ions. Fig. 7 exhibits the variation of current efficiency with time for the experiments carried out at the applied voltage of 7 V. It is seen that current efficiency was enhanced with decreasing feed concentration. Once the feed concentration has decreased, it leads to lowered lithium ions permeation flux and therefore a smaller change in the number of ions in the feed compartment. On the other hand, the reduction in the electrical current and therefore the number of electrical charges passing the cell during a specific time is smaller than the charges transferred by the permeated ions and, therefore, current efficiency becomes greater by decreasing the feed concentration. Obtaining current efficiency values larger than 100% can be explained by considering the molecular diffusion through the cation exchange membrane, which was due in large part to the difference between electrolyte concentrations in the feed and cathodic compartments. This finding was in agreement with the results reported by others (Luo et al., 2002; Wu et al., 2011).

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 4. Conclusions
texts.text: 4. Conclusions

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In this research, electrodialysis process was used to recover lithium ions from a contaminated solution of lithium bromide. The influences of some operating parameters such as applied voltage, initial feed concentration in the recovery of lithium hydroxide, and specific electricity consumption were investigated, and the following results were obtained:
texts.text: In this research, electrodialysis process was used to recover lithium ions from a contaminated solution of lithium bromide. The influences of some operating parameters such as applied voltage, initial feed concentration in the recovery of lithium hydroxide, and specific electricity consumption were investigated, and the following results were obtained:

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: 1. Approximately 20% of lithium ions were recovered in the form of lithium hydroxide from the contaminated lithium bromide solution. The resultant LiOH solution containing a low concentration of sodium ions can be used for many applications, including Li-ion batteries and reproducing LiBr solution.
texts.text: Approximately 20% of lithium ions were recovered in the form of lithium hydroxide from the contaminated lithium bromide solution. The resultant LiOH solution containing a low concentration of sodium ions can be used for many applications, including Li-ion batteries and reproducing LiBr solution.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: 2. Applying higher levels of voltage had increasing effects on all parameters of lithium ions permeation, lithium hydroxide concentration, and the ratio of lithium-to-sodium ion concentration in the cathodic chamber, and SEC.
texts.text: Applying higher levels of voltage had increasing effects on all parameters of lithium ions permeation, lithium hydroxide concentration, and the ratio of lithium-to-sodium ion concentration in the cathodic chamber, and SEC.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: 3. Increasing the initial lithium ion concentration in the feed solution led to larger values for lithium ions' permeation flux. On the other hand, lowered values were observed for lithium ions' recovery, the ratio of lithium-to-sodium ions' concentration in the cathodic chamber, current efficiency, and SEC by increasing the initial lithium ions concentration in the feed solution.
texts.text: Increasing the initial lithium ion concentration in the feed solution led to larger values for lithium ions' permeation flux. On the other hand, lowered values were observed for lithium ions' recovery, the ratio of lithium-to-sodium ions' concentration in the cathodic chamber, current efficiency, and SEC by increasing the initial lithium ions concentration in the feed solution.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: 4. Finally, the obtained results proved that the implemented process could be considered a promising method in avoiding the waste of a large volume of contaminated LiBr solution. The highest obtained concentration of lithium ions in the cathodic compartment was 7654 ppm. This was accompanied with 105 ppm of sodium ions. Once lithium ions are considered as LiOH, the above mentioned concentrations introduce a LiOH solution with 0.4 wt% of sodium contaminant. According to these findings, lithium ions can be recovered as highly pure lithium hydroxide solution with many potential applications.
